attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman is admitted to the labour ward with spontaneous rupture of membranes. This is her first pregnancy and she is at 40 weeks gestation. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has not had a 36 week GBS swab.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 80/min, blood pressure 120/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Abdominal examination reveals a longitudinal lie with cephalic presentation. The fetal heart rate is 140 beats per minute with good variability. Speculum examination confirms clear amniotic fluid and reveals that her cervix is long and closed. She is not experiencing any contractions.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Offer induction of labour with oxytocin infusion within 24 hours
Offer induction of labour with artificial rupture of membranes
Offer induction of labour with balloon catheter for cervical ripening
Offer expectant management for at least 72 hours
attributes.explanation:
In this scenario, the woman has spontaneous rupture of membranes (SROM) at term but is not in labour. The most appropriate next step is to start oxytocin infusion within 24 hours. This is the preferred method for induction of labour in women with ruptured membranes at term who are not in labour, especially when the cervix is unfavourable (long and closed).

Artificial rupture of membranes is not applicable in this case as the membranes have already ruptured spontaneously. This option is irrelevant in the current scenario.

An immediate caesarean section would be an overly aggressive approach at this stage. While labour has not started, there are no signs of fetal distress or other complications that would necessitate an immediate caesarean section. Induction of labour should be attempted first in this situation.

Inserting a balloon catheter for cervical ripening is not recommended in cases of ruptured membranes due to the increased risk of infection. While mechanical methods like balloon catheters are often used for cervical ripening in cases of an unfavourable cervix when membranes are intact, the risk of infection outweighs the potential benefits in this scenario of ruptured membranes.

Expectant management is not recommended in this case. Given that the membranes have already ruptured, prolonged expectant management increases the risk of ascending infection. Active management with oxytocin induction within 24 hours is indicated to reduce the time to delivery and minimize the risk of chorioamnionitis is what RANZCOG guidelines recommend.

Oxytocin infusion stimulates uterine contractions and is the standard method for inducing labour in women with ruptured membranes who are not contracting, even when the cervix is unfavourable. It's important to note that continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is essential during oxytocin-induced labour due to the increased risk of uterine hyperstimulation and fetal distress. Regular assessment of uterine contractions and cervical dilatation is also crucial to ensure appropriate progress and avoid complications. The oxytocin dose should be titrated carefully to achieve adequate contractions without overstimulating the uterus.
learningPoints: Oxytocin infusion within 24 hours is the preferred method for induction of labour in women with ruptured membranes at term who are not in labour.
Continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is essential during oxytocin-induced labour due to the increased risk of uterine hyperstimulation and fetal distress.
Regular assessment of uterine contractions and cervical dilatation is crucial during oxytocin-induced labour to ensure appropriate progress and avoid complications.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Term prelabour rupture of membranes (Term PROM) [Internet]. East Melbourne (Australia): RANZCOG; 2017. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Term-Prelabour-Rupture-of-Membranes-Term-PROM.pdf

Royal Women’s Hospital. Rupture of membranes at term: pre-labour midwifery assessment and management - procedure. Version 2.1. Melbourne: Royal Women’s Hospital; 2024. Available from: https://prod-prompt-documents.s3.ap-southeast-2.amazonaws.com/192721/192721_v2.1.pdf
